title: Charmed Life - Chapter 13 (Fragment 1)
text:
Chapter Ten

10

C
 AT WATCHED his moment and, when Mr. Saunders called them to lessons, he caught Roger’s arm and whispered, “Look, Gwendolen’s turned Euphemia into a frog and—”

Roger gave a great snore of laughter. Cat had to wait for him to stop.

“And she won’t turn her back. Can you ?”

Roger tried to look serious, but laughter kept breaking through. “I don’t know. Probably not, unless she’ll tell you what spell she used. Finding out which spell without knowing is Advanced Magic, and I’m not on that yet. Oh, how funny!” He bent over the table and yelled with laughter.

Naturally, Mr. Saunders appeared at the door, remarking that the time for telling jokes was after lessons. They had to go through to the schoolroom. Naturally, Cat found Janet had sat in his desk by mistake. He got her out as quietly as he could and sat in it himself, distractedly wondering how he could find out which spell Gwendolen had used.

It was the most uncomfortable morning Cat had ever known. He had forgotten to tell Janet that the only thing Gwendolen knew about was witchcraft. Janet, as he had rather suspected, knew a lot, about a lot of things. But it all applied to her own world. About the only subject she would have been safe in was simple arithmetic. And Mr. Saunders chose that morning to give her a History test. Cat, as he scratched away left-handed at an English essay, could see the panic growing on Janet’s face.

“What do you mean, Henry the Fifth?” barked Mr. Saunders. “Richard the Second was on the throne until long after Agincourt. What was his greatest magical achievement?”

“Defeating the French,” Janet guessed. Mr. Saunders looked so exasperated that she babbled, “Well, I think it was. He hampered the French with iron underwear, and the English wore wool, so they didn’t stick in the mud, and probably their longbows were enchanted too. That would account for them not missing.”

“Who,” said Mr. Saunders, “do you imagine won the Battle of Agincourt?”

“The English,” said Janet. This of course was true for her world, but the panic-stricken look on her face as she said it suggested that she suspected the opposite was true in this world. Which, of course, it was.

Mr. Saunders put his hands to his head. “No, no, no! The French ! Don’t you know anything , girl?”

Janet looked to be near tears. Cat was terrified. She was going to break down any second and tell Mr. Saunders she was not Gwendolen. She did not have Cat’s reasons for keeping quiet. “Gwendolen never knows anything,” he remarked loudly, hoping Janet would take the hint. She did. She sighed with relief and relaxed.

“I’m aware of that,” said Mr. Saunders. “But somewhere, somewhere inside that marble head there must be a little cell of gray matter. So I keep looking.”

Unfortunately, Janet, in her relief, became almost jolly. “Would you like to take my head apart and look?” she asked.

“Don’t tempt me!” cried Mr. Saunders. He hid his eyes with one knobby hand and fended at Janet with the other. He looked so funny that Janet laughed. This was so unlike Gwendolen that Mr. Saunders lowered his hand across his nose and stared at her suspiciously over it. “What have you been up to now?”

“Nothing,” Janet said guiltily.

“Hm,” said Mr. Saunders, in a way which made both Cat and Janet very uncomfortable.

vocabulary.definition: At a higher or more developed stage; complex or requiring great knowledge.
vocabulary.example: Finding out which spell without knowing is Advanced Magic.

vocabulary.definition: The branch of mathematics dealing with numbers and the basic operations on them.
vocabulary.example: She was safe in simple arithmetic.

vocabulary.definition: To make an effort to achieve or complete something, often difficult.
vocabulary.example: He tried to look serious.

vocabulary.definition: To talk rapidly and continuously in a foolish, excited, or incomprehensible way.
vocabulary.example: She babbled, “Well, I think it was.”

vocabulary.definition: A small part of the brain tissue responsible for processing thoughts and intelligence.
vocabulary.example: A little cell of gray matter inside that marble head.

vocabulary.definition: Unable to concentrate because one's attention is diverted.
vocabulary.example: He sat distractedly wondering.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that shows a lack of attention or concentration.
vocabulary.example: He sat in it himself, distractedly wondering how he could find out which spell Gwendolen had used.

vocabulary.definition: Placed under a magical spell; delighted or filled with wonder.
vocabulary.example: Their longbows were enchanted too.

vocabulary.definition: Intensely irritated or frustrated.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Saunders looked so exasperated that she babbled.

vocabulary.definition: To confront or deal with a difficult situation or person.
vocabulary.example: The panic growing on Janet’s face.

vocabulary.definition: To ward off or repel, especially by blocking or parrying.
vocabulary.example: He fended at Janet with the other hand.

vocabulary.definition: To hinder or impede the movement or progress of something.
vocabulary.example: He hampered the French with iron underwear.

vocabulary.definition: Happy and cheerful.
vocabulary.example: Janet, in her relief, became almost jolly.

vocabulary.definition: Having many knobs or lumps; lump-like.
vocabulary.example: He hid his eyes with one knobby hand.

translations.ru: мраморная голова (здесь: упрямый или глупый человек)

vocabulary.definition: A metaphorical expression implying a person has a hard, unchanging mind or limited understanding.
vocabulary.example: Inside that marble head there must be a little cell of gray matter.

vocabulary.definition: Very wonderful or amazing, like a miracle.
Overcome or affected by sudden uncontrollable fear or anxiety.
vocabulary.example: The panic-stricken look on her face suggested she suspected the opposite was true.

vocabulary.definition: Being free from tension or anxiety.
vocabulary.example: She sighed with relief and relaxed.

vocabulary.definition: To say something as a comment or observation.
vocabulary.example: She did not have Cat’s reasons for keeping quiet,” he remarked loudly.

vocabulary.definition: A loud sound made during sleep or, metaphorically, a loud laugh or noise.
vocabulary.example: Roger gave a great snore of laughter.

vocabulary.definition: A series of words that have magical power when spoken or written.
vocabulary.example: Can you find out which spell she used?

vocabulary.definition: To have an idea or impression of the existence or truth of something without certain proof.
vocabulary.example: Janet, as he had rather suspected, knew a lot.

vocabulary.definition: Extremely frightened.
vocabulary.example: Cat was terrified.

vocabulary.definition: An examination of knowledge or proficiency.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Saunders chose that morning to give her a History test.

vocabulary.definition: To speak very softly using one's breath without vocal cords, especially for privacy or secrecy.
vocabulary.example: He whispered, “Look, Gwendolen’s turned Euphemia into a frog and—”

vocabulary.definition: To shout something very loudly.
vocabulary.example: He bent over the table and yelled with laughter.
